figures.caption: Figure 1: Proposed network architecture and multi-phase training strategy. Phase 1 (Steady-State): Fully connected blocks (FNN1, FNN3) map spatial inputs x to a steady-state baseline us,ϕ(x), optimized via data or physics-informed residual loss. Phase 2 (Transient): Temporal inputs t and spatial features are merged to capture the space-time correction gθ(x, t). The combined spatio-temporal solution uh(x, t) is then trained using a time-dependent physics-informed loss. Initial and boundary conditions are enforced as soft constraints (omitted from the diagram for brevity).

Figure 2: One-dimensional heat equation: Extrapolation performance and temporal function f(t). (a) Relative L2 error over time for the temperature field, evaluated across various temporal functions f(t). The network is trained on t ∈ [0, 0.5] and extrapolated to t = 5. (b) Comparison of the learned temporal profiles f(t).

Figure 3: One-dimensional heat equation: Extrapolated solution versus the reference solution for f(t) = e−λt at (a) t = 1 and (b) t = 2. The network is trained on t ∈ [0, 0.5]. The initial condition u(x, 0) and the steady-state solution us(x) are shown for reference.
Figure 5: Lid-Driven Cavity Flow: L∞ error over time for the velocity components (a) u1 and (b) u2, evaluated across various temporal functions f(t), with the network trained on t ∈ [0, 0.5].
Figure 6: Lid-Driven Cavity Flow: L∞ error over time for the velocity components (a) u1 and (b) u2, evaluated across various temporal functions f(t), with the network trained on t ∈ [0, 1].

Figure 7: Lid-Driven Cavity Flow: Velocity predictions and pointwise absolute errors at t = 1.5 for f(t) = e−λt, with the model trained on t ∈ [0, 0.5]. (a) Horizontal velocity uh 1 . (b) Absolute error |u1 − uh 1 |. (c) Vertical velocity uh 2 . (d) Absolute error |u2 − uh 2 |.

Figure 8: Lid-Driven Cavity Flow: Contour plots showing the absolute spatial differences in the velocity components u1 and u2. Panels (a) and (b) show the evolution of velocity between t = 0.5 and t = 1.5, while panels (c) and (d) show the evolution of the velocity between t = 1.5 and t = 9.

Figure 9: Natural convection in a square cavity: (a) L∞ error over time for the temperature field, evaluated across various temporal functions f(t). The network is trained on t ∈ [0, 10] and extrapolated to t = 100. (b) The learned temporal functions for f(t)

Figure 10: Natural convection in a square cavity: Temporal evolution of temperature profiles (top row) and the corresponding point-wise absolute errors of the temperature predictions (bottom row) with f(t) = e−λt(a+ cos(ωt)).
Figure 11: Three-dimensional domain for conjugate heat transfer

Figure 12: Conjugate heat transfer in three dimensions: (a) Relative L2 error over time for the temperature field, evaluated across various temporal functions f(t). The network is trained on t ∈ [0, 1.5] and extrapolated to t = 10. All the networks FNNr i , where i = {1, 2, 3, 4} and r = {f, s}, are trained with physics-informed losses. (b) The learned temporal functions f(t).

Figure 13: Conjugate heat transfer in three dimensions: (a) Relative L2 error over time for the temperature field, evaluated across various temporal functions f(t). The network is trained on t ∈ [0, 1.5] and extrapolated to t = 10. FNNr 1,3 are trained with FEM solutions and FNNr 2,4 are trained with physics-informed losses, where r = {f, s}. (b) The learned temporal functions f(t).

Figure 14: Conjugate heat transfer in three dimensions: Temporal evolution of temperature profiles (top row) on a YZ plane at x = 0.5 and the corresponding point-wise absolute errors of the temperature predictions (bottom row) with f(t) = e−λt(a+ cos(ωt)).

Figure 15: Conjugate heat transfer in three dimensions: The top row illustrates the temporal evolution of temperature profiles and the accompanying velocity field on the YZ-plane (x = 0.5) and XZ-plane (y = 0.5). The bottom row displays the corresponding point-wise absolute errors for the temperature predictions with f(t) = e−λt(a+ cos(ωt)).
